title-group.article-title: The Self‐Care Paradox: From Nursing Theory to Neoliberal Subjectivity

article-meta.aff: 1 College of Nursing, Chungnam National University, Daejeon, Korea

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Nursing Inquiry published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Self‐care occupies a central, often taken‐for‐granted and normatively privileged position in contemporary nursing discourse, consistently framed as a marker of empowerment, autonomy and responsible health behaviour. Although critical scholarship has contested this framing through work on relational care, feminist critiques of individualism and the neoliberal responsibilisation of health, the normative work self‐care performs within nursing's own conceptual frameworks remains insufficiently examined. This paper offers a theoretical analysis of self‐care as a subject‐forming discourse. It revisits Orem's Self‐Care Deficit Nursing Theory as a conceptual baseline in which vulnerability and dependence are ethically acceptable conditions that legitimately call forth nursing intervention, and traces how neoliberal health discourse has reconfigured self‐care from capacity into obligation. Building on existing structural critiques, the paper argues that a further, subjective transformation warrants attention: self‐care discourse contributes to the formation of subject positions in which responsibility is internalised, vulnerability experienced as inadequacy and care‐seeking as personal failure. These subject positions are unevenly taken up across class, gender, age and health status, and may be resisted or reinterpreted. Drawing on Byung‐Chul Han's account of the achievement subject, the paper conceptualises this dynamic here as the self‐care paradox and considers what it asks of nursing theory.

sec.p: Keywords: neoliberalism, nursing theory, self‐care, self‐care paradox, subjectivity, vulnerability
Revised 2026 Apr 27; Received 2026 Mar 20; Accepted 2026 May 25; Issue date 2026 Jul.

sec.title: The Problem That Nursing Theory Cannot Yet Name
sec.p: Nurses routinely encounter a particular kind of patient: one who insists they are managing when they are not, who minimises their needs and who apologises for requiring care. These presentations are typically addressed as problems of communication, motivation or health literacy. They are rarely recognised for what they may also be: the subjective effects of a discourse that has made self‐sufficiency a moral standard and help‐seeking its implicit failure.

To illustrate this dynamic, consider two brief, composite illustrations. An 82‐year‐old woman living alone with heart failure, chronic kidney disease and advancing frailty keeps her district nurse's visits brief by reporting that she is ‘coping fine’. She has reorganised her life around the concealment of difficulty: she weighs herself at the same time each day, records her symptoms accurately for her own reassurance and speaks about her condition in the cheerful, managerial register she has learned from years of clinical encounters. She will not mention the falls she has had, nor the neighbour who has started bringing her groceries, because doing so would mark her as no longer capable of self‐management. A second patient, in his forties and in recovery from opioid dependence, attends his scheduled appointments reliably and speaks fluently about his coping strategies. When his cravings intensify, he does not contact his care team. To do so, he tells himself, would be to admit that he had failed at the task he was supposed to have mastered; the moral grammar of recovery, in which relapse reads as personal collapse, makes the request for additional support feel like a confession of inadequacy. In neither case is the withholding irrational or unreflective. In both cases, it is one form in which patients may come to understand what competent self‐care requires of them.

Self‐care occupies a central and normatively privileged position in contemporary nursing discourse. Across theory, education and practice, it is consistently invoked as an unambiguous good: a marker of empowerment, autonomy and responsible health behaviour. Although a body of critical scholarship, including work on relational care, feminist critiques of individualism and the neoliberal responsibilisation of health, has contested this framing, the normative work self‐care performs within nursing's own conceptual frameworks remains insufficiently examined: what it enables, what it forecloses and whose claims to care it renders legitimate or suspect. And when nursing theory has no language for that normative work, it cannot see what it is participating in.

sec.title: From Structural Critique to the Question of the Subject
sec.p: The ethical dimensions of self‐care discourse have not gone entirely unnoticed. Greaney and Flaherty (2020) have argued that contemporary self‐care agendas risk functioning as care left undone, redistributing responsibility away from systems and institutions and onto individuals who may be ill‐equipped to bear it. This structural critique is important, and the present paper takes it as a necessary point of departure.

Yet structural critique alone cannot fully account for a more troubling dynamic. Analyses that locate the problem in the redistribution of responsibility (e.g., Brown 2015; Greaney and Flaherty 2020; Lupton 2016; A. Petersen and Lupton 2000; Rose 2001) implicitly retain a distinction between the structural pressures that shape individuals and the individuals who bear them. These pressures include institutional performance metrics, conditional access to services and welfare provision, workload intensification and the health‐system expectation that patients demonstrate responsibility for their own health behaviours. Such analyses describe a world in which people are burdened, constrained or failed by systems, yet in which the subject remains, in principle, separable from the discourse that governs them. What they do not fully address is the possibility that self‐care discourse does not merely confront subjects as an external set of institutional expectations, but is internalised such that these expectations come to feel like the subject's own.

This is where the paradox becomes visible. When self‐care is internalised as a standard of moral adequacy, when failing to be self‐caring is experienced not as an unmet external obligation but as personal inadequacy, then the subject is not simply governed by self‐care discourse. The subject has become its instrument. And it is precisely at this point that the paradox crystallises: the discourse designed to empower individuals to care for themselves becomes the mechanism through which seeking care from others is foreclosed, not by prohibition, but by the subject's own self‐regulation.

This is a nursing problem before it is a theoretical one. Nursing is among the discourses that most directly and consistently addresses self‐care to patients, and it does so through multiple, mutually reinforcing channels: canonical nursing theories of self‐care, standardised assessment frameworks, care‐planning templates, patient education materials and the routine language of bedside encounters. Each of these carries implicit normative expectations about what counts as adequate self‐management, and together they constitute a pedagogical environment in which seeking help can come to register, for some patients, as moral shortfall rather than as a legitimate response to limitation. If that address participates in forming subjects who cannot seek care, then nursing theory must account for what it is doing. This paper argues that it cannot do so without first naming what has not yet been adequately named: the self‐care paradox and the kind of subject it produces.

sec.title: Aim, Approach and the Case for Theoretical Intervention
sec.p: This paper offers a theoretical and philosophical analysis of self‐care as a subject‐forming discourse: one that does not merely reorganise responsibility at the systemic level, but reshapes how individuals understand their own needs, limitations and entitlement to care. It is, deliberately, a theoretical paper. This is not to suggest that empirical inquiry cannot reach the phenomenon in question; qualitative research (phenomenological, narrative and ethnographic) has documented many of the experiences our argument addresses, including unreported fears, withheld needs and the moral weight patients attach to help‐seeking. The conceptual analysis developed here is complementary to such work rather than a substitute for it: it aims to illuminate the discursive conditions under which these experiences become expressible or inexpressible in clinical encounters. The phenomenon the paper addresses, the internalisation of self‐care as a standard of moral adequacy, is often most legible not in what patients report, but in what they do not: the needs they have learned to regard as inadmissible, the vulnerability they experience as defeat. To name this requires a theoretical framework capable of attending to the interior of self‐governance. That is what this paper attempts to provide.

Existing critiques of neoliberal health discourse have documented how self‐care has been reconfigured from a capacity into an obligation, redistributing responsibility from systems onto individuals (Brown 2015; Cederstrom and Spicer 2015; Crawford 1980; Greaney and Flaherty 2020; Lupton 1995; Rose 2001). This paper extends that critique by attending to a further transformation that operates at the subjective register: self‐care discourse does not merely redistribute responsibility onto individuals; it contributes to the formation of subjects who redistribute it onto themselves, experiencing their own vulnerability as inadequacy and their need for care as personal failure. This is the self‐care paradox, and it is a dynamic that structural critique alone cannot fully reach.

The analytical approach adopted here draws on two complementary theoretical resources. It resonates with Foucauldian accounts of subjectification and technologies of the self, in which subjects are constituted through discursive practices they come to enact upon themselves (Foucault 1988). Where Foucault's framework foregrounds the productive and dispersed operations of power, however, this paper draws primarily on Byung‐Chul Han's (2015) account of the achievement subject, which attends more directly to the subjective experience of internalised demand: exhaustion, self‐erasure and the foreclosure of vulnerability. The two frameworks are not incompatible; Han's analysis brings into focus what Foucauldian critique tends to leave in the background, namely, the felt interior of self‐governance.

To develop this argument, the paper proceeds in four stages. It first revisits Dorothea Orem's self‐care theory to establish a conceptual baseline in which dependence and care‐seeking are ethically legitimate conditions rather than failures to be overcome. It then examines how neoliberal health discourse has reconfigured self‐care from capacity to obligation, and names the tension that follows as the self‐care paradox. The analysis then draws on social–theoretical resources, particularly accounts of the achievement society and the internalisation of performance demands, to illuminate how this paradox operates within the subject, as a self‐imposed foreclosure of care. Finally, it considers what this analysis asks of nursing theory: not a new model of self‐care, but a more honest reckoning with what nursing's own discourse does when it enters the interior lives of the people it exists to care for.

sec.title: Self‐Care as a Canonical Concept in Nursing
Orem's Self‐Care Theory: Capacity, Deficit and the Legitimacy of Dependence
sec.p: Several seminal nursing theories of self‐care have shaped the field's conceptual landscape. Pender's Health Promotion Model (Pender et al. 2015) centres individual agency in the pursuit of positive health outcomes, foregrounding perceived benefits, self‐efficacy and personal commitment as determinants of health‐promoting behaviour. Riegel and colleagues' middle‐range theory of self‐care of chronic illness (Riegel et al. 2012, 2019) articulates self‐care as a process of maintenance, monitoring and management enacted under the conditions of long‐term illness. Each of these frameworks engages questions of self‐management and agency, and each has informed substantial programmes of clinical research and intervention design. The present paper selects Orem's Self‐Care Deficit Nursing Theory (Orem et al. 1995) as its primary point of reference for three reasons. First, Orem's account is the most explicit and internally elaborated among these frameworks in specifying what the self‐caring subject is, what counts as a self‐care deficit and when nursing intervention is legitimately called forth. Second, Orem treats self‐care deficit as the ethical ground of nursing rather than as a signal of failure, which makes her framework unusually well‐positioned to illuminate the transformation this paper traces. Third, because Orem's theory explicitly theorises the ‘self‐care agent’, it renders visible the normative subject whose reconfiguration under neoliberal health discourse is the concern of this analysis. The argument that follows does not depend on Orem's theory being superior to these alternatives; it depends on Orem providing the clearest conceptual baseline against which a subsequent historical transformation can be legibly drawn.

In Orem's self‐care theory, self‐care is conceptualised as a capacity (the ability of individuals to engage in activities necessary for the maintenance of life, health and well‐being) whose limitation or absence is understood as an expected feature of human life (Orem et al. 1995). Self‐care deficit does not signify failure or moral inadequacy. Illness, developmental stage and situational constraint may render individuals temporarily or persistently unable to meet their own self‐care needs, and Orem treats such deficits as the normal conditions that give rise to the need for nursing.

Within this framework, vulnerability is not a deviation from the norm but an ordinary condition of human life, and dependence is not morally suspect. Nursing intervention is warranted not because individuals ought to be self‐sufficient, but because self‐care capacity is inherently limited and contingent. Requesting assistance is understood as a reasonable response to recognised limitation, and nursing practice is explicitly oriented toward affirming that response as both acceptable and professionally warranted (Orem et al. 1995).

sec.title: Orem's Subject and What Follows
sec.p: What is most significant for the present analysis is the specific form that Orem's ethical orientation takes. In this framework, the self‐care deficit is the condition that calls forth care, not as a concession to failure, but as a recognition of the ordinary limits of human capacity. Vulnerability does not disqualify a subject from care; it is precisely what entitles them to it.1 It is against this baseline that the transformation examined in this paper becomes legible: what happens when that subject is reconstructed, when vulnerability becomes not the ground of entitlement, but evidence of inadequacy, and when the self‐care deficit ceases to justify care and begins instead to justify its withdrawal.

sec.title: The Neoliberal Reconfiguration of Self‐Care
sec.p: Within neoliberal health discourse, health is reframed as the outcome of personal decisions and self‐regulatory behaviours rather than as a condition shaped by structural relations or collective arrangements (Rose 2001; Harvey 2005; Al‐Chami 2025). Risk is progressively individualised: illness and vulnerability are no longer understood primarily as contingent or situational phenomena, but as foreseeable conditions that individuals are expected to anticipate, manage and minimise through appropriate self‐care practices (Crawford 1980; Lupton 1995; A. Petersen and Lupton 2000; Cairns and Johnston 2015). Freedom is redefined as the capacity to choose responsibly, where choice is inseparable from accountability for its consequences. Health becomes a moralised domain in which individuals are evaluated not only by clinical criteria but by their demonstrated willingness to self‐manage, and the subject of neoliberal health governance is, above all, one whose claim to care is conditioned on evidence of having taken responsibility (Trnka and Trundle 2014; Brown 2015; N. Petersen and Schicktanz 2021).

It is within this context that self‐care undergoes a significant transformation. What Orem conceptualised as a contingent capacity, something that may or may not be achievable depending on circumstance, is progressively recast as a standing obligation. Self‐care deficits, once the legitimate ground for nursing intervention, are reframed as signals of self‐management failure. Dependence, once ethically acceptable, becomes morally suspect: evidence of insufficient effort or poor choices (Rimke 2000). Self‐care shifts from a descriptive and relational concept to an evaluative and disciplinary one: a standard against which individuals are measured, and through which their entitlement to care is adjudicated. For nursing, this shift has a precise consequence, repositioning nurses not only as providers of care but as assessors of whether patients have sufficiently earned it.

sec.title: From External Discourse to Interior Demand
sec.p: Structural critiques of neoliberal health governance have documented this transformation with considerable sophistication (Greaney and Flaherty 2020; Rose 2001). What they have not fully addressed is the next step: what happens when the discourse is not merely imposed on subjects but absorbed by them. When self‐care is consistently framed as a marker of competence and moral worth, its demands do not remain external. They become the standards by which individuals evaluate their own adequacy: the inner voice that registers a failure to self‐manage not as an understandable limitation, but as a personal deficit. The reconfiguration of self‐care from capacity to obligation does not only alter how patients are perceived by systems and institutions. It alters how patients perceive themselves. And it is this interior transformation, not the redistribution of responsibility, but its internalisation, that the following sections examine.

This transformation, however, does not unfold uniformly across contexts or individuals. Patients draw on collective, familial and culturally specific resources to resist, reinterpret or sidestep the obligation to optimise. Some frame dependence through relational or faith‐based understandings in which reliance on others is not a moral failure but a constitutive feature of belonging. Others mobilise personal insight or lived experience of illness to contest the binary of self‐sufficiency and failure. The subject position described in the sections that follow is therefore best understood as one significant formation among others, whose uptake is socially differentiated and whose grip on individual experience varies with class, gender, age, cultural location and access to supportive relationships. Attending to how this formation operates is not to deny these variations, but to name a dynamic that, where it does take hold, exerts considerable force on how care is sought and withheld.

sec.title: The Self‐Care Paradox
sec.p: This paper names the tension traced in the preceding sections as the self‐care paradox: the counterintuitive dynamic whereby the intensified promotion of self‐care simultaneously renders care‐seeking more difficult. The paradox does not lie in a simple contradiction between self‐care and care. It lies in a structural inversion: the elevation of self‐care as a moral standard produces a normative environment in which dependence (the very condition that Orem identified as the legitimate ground for nursing intervention) becomes evidence of inadequacy. What was once the justification for care becomes, in this reconfigured discourse, a reason for subjects to withhold it from themselves.

sec.title: Two Registers of the Paradox
sec.p: The self‐care paradox operates at two distinct but related registers, and it is important to distinguish them. At the first register, the structural, the paradox is visible in how self‐care discourse reshapes the conditions under which care is provided and received. When self‐care is framed as a standing obligation, self‐care deficits are no longer read as calls for assistance but as signals of failure. Individuals who are unable to meet normative expectations of self‐management may hesitate to seek help, anticipating judgement, withdrawal of support or the implicit accusation of irresponsibility. This is the terrain that structural critiques of neoliberal health governance have begun to map (Greaney and Flaherty 2020).

Two brief examples illustrate how this structural register operates in everyday clinical practice and reshapes the conditions under which care is provided and received. In chronic disease self‐management programmes, adherence metrics (medication counts, blood glucose logs and attendance at follow‐up appointments) simultaneously enable support and produce a register in which difficulty becomes legible as personal failure. For some patients, readings that drift outside target ranges do not simply indicate that their physiology is responding poorly; within the terms of the encounter, they become someone who has not done enough. The measurement practice that was meant to facilitate intervention also sorts patients into those who are ‘engaged’ and those who are ‘non‐adherent’, and the latter category carries moral weight that the former does not. A second example can be found in discharge planning conversations. When clinicians ask a patient whether they will be ‘able to manage at home’, the question is rarely neutral: a confident affirmative expedites discharge, reduces pressure on beds and meets institutional performance targets, while an expression of doubt may delay release and invite further assessment. Patients quickly learn which answer moves the encounter forward, and many give it regardless of how they actually feel. In both cases, the structural conditions of contemporary care do not merely ration resources; they shape what becomes sayable about needing care in the first place.

At the second register, the subjective, the paradox takes a more consequential and less visible form. Here, the problem is not that individuals fear external judgement, but that they have internalised the standards by which that judgement operates. When self‐care has been absorbed as a measure of one's own moral worth, the inability to self‐manage is experienced not as an unmet external demand, but as personal inadequacy. At this register, the foreclosure of care‐seeking is not produced by institutional barriers or social stigma alone. It is produced by the subject's own self‐assessment, by the voice that says: I should be able to manage this myself.

sec.title: The Paradox as a Problem for Nursing Theory
sec.p: It is the second register, the subjective, that existing analyses have left undertheorised and that this paper is primarily concerned with. The result is a subject who does not merely face barriers to care but who has, in a meaningful sense, become a barrier to their own care. Dependence is not refused because it is forbidden; it is refused because it has come to feel like defeat.

This is not a claim about individual failure. The subjects described here are not irresponsible, nor are they unaware of their needs. They are subjects formed, through the repeated address of self‐care discourse, such that vulnerability feels like a problem to be solved rather than a condition to be responded to. For nursing, this matters precisely because it means the barrier to care‐seeking is not external but interior: not something that better access or clearer information can address, but something produced within the subject that nursing's own discourse may be helping to create.

sec.title: The Achievement Subject
sec.p: Han (2015) argues that contemporary societies are increasingly organised not around prohibition but around achievement. The crucial shift is this: power operates not through discipline imposed from outside but through self‐imposed expectations that individuals actively embrace as expressions of their own freedom. When the imperative to perform is experienced as self‐generated, failure becomes a verdict on the self rather than a response to circumstance; exhaustion registers not as a signal that too much has been asked, but as evidence that not enough has been given.2 It is precisely this mechanism, internalised demand experienced as autonomous will, that allows self‐care to function as the paper argues it does: not as an obligation imposed on subjects, but as one they impose on themselves.

The logic Han describes is not specific to health. Parallel dynamics are visible across contemporary work, education and productivity cultures. In the workplace, the language of self‐optimisation, resilience and ‘owning’ one's development has displaced earlier vocabularies of workload and labour relations (Cederstrom and Spicer 2015); the employee who falls behind can come to be read as no longer simply overworked but insufficiently self‐managing, and wellness programmes that measure sleep, steps and stress paradoxically extend the reach of performance metrics into the very domains once reserved for recovery. In education, the contemporary learner is expected to take ownership of their own progress, manage their own time and treat study as an ongoing project of self‐improvement; difficulty is refigured as an individual deficit of discipline or motivation rather than a structural feature of the curriculum or the conditions under which learning takes place. Across these domains, the common thread is a shift from demands that are recognised as external to demands that are taken up as expressions of the subject's own will, and, correspondingly, a reconfiguration of struggle as self‐failure rather than as a signal that the conditions of work, study or health have become unreasonable. Self‐care, understood in the terms this paper develops, may be understood as one instance of a broader logic rather than an isolated phenomenon of the health field.

sec.title: Self‐Care Absorbed Into the Logic of Performance
sec.p: It is within this structure that self‐care undergoes a further transformation, one that goes beyond the discursive reconfiguration described in Section 3. Self‐care does not merely become an obligation imposed from outside; it becomes a project that the achievement subject takes up as their own.

The language through which self‐care is increasingly articulated reveals this absorption. Contemporary self‐care discourse is saturated with the vocabulary of performance: resilience, optimisation, wellness and capacity maintenance (Cederstrom and Spicer 2015). Rest is reframed not as a response to need but as a recovery strategy: a technique for restoring the functional capacity required to continue performing. Sleep becomes optimised. Exercise becomes a productivity tool. Emotional regulation becomes a competency. In each case, the orientation of care shifts: it is no longer directed toward vulnerability or need, but toward the preservation and enhancement of performance.

This is not a metaphorical overlap between care and performance but a structural transformation in what self‐care is for. Within the achievement society's logic, caring for oneself means maintaining the self as productive and self‐sufficient: managing vulnerability, minimising it and where possible eliminating it before it requires the involvement of others.

sec.title: The Refusal of Vulnerability From Within
sec.p: This is where the self‐care paradox reaches its sharpest subjective form. The achievement subject, to borrow Han's (2015) term, is characterised less by what external authorities prohibit than by what internalised imperatives demand. Where earlier configurations of power worked through command and denial, the achievement subject is positioned as apparently free, self‐directed and responsible for their own optimisation. The corresponding reconfiguration of failure is decisive: falling short no longer registers as a transgression against external rule but as self‐exhaustion, evidence of inadequate commitment to one's own becoming. Applied to the domain of health, this subject does not merely face external barriers to care‐seeking. They face an internal barrier: the sense that to need care is to have failed at self‐care.

Han's account of burnout is illuminating here, not as a clinical category but as a subjective experience that reveals the limits of achievement logic. Burnout, in Han's analysis, is not the result of too much external demand. It is the result of a subject who has identified so completely with the demand to perform that they cannot stop, even when they are exhausted, even when they are unwell, even when the cost of continuing is the self itself (Han 2015). Depression, similarly, is not a withdrawal from demand but a collapse under the weight of a demand that has become indistinguishable from the self. Ehrenberg's (2010) analysis of depression as the characteristic illness of a society that has made self‐determination its sovereign norm offers a parallel and complementary diagnosis: what presents as psychological breakdown is also the breakdown of a particular model of the subject. More recent work extends this analysis by situating Han's account within the broader literature on neoliberal psychopolitics, arguing that the achievement subject is produced through technologies of self‐governance that operate beneath the threshold of conscious recognition (Alphin and Debrix 2023). Taken together, these accounts point to the same structure: the subject who cannot stop, cannot ask for help and cannot acknowledge need, not because they are prohibited from doing so, but because doing so would constitute a verdict on the self that the self cannot bear.

Translated into the terms of this paper: the subject formed by self‐care discourse does not hesitate to seek help because they fear judgement. They hesitate because seeking help has come to feel like a confession: an admission that they have not managed themselves adequately, that they have allowed vulnerability to become visible and that they have failed the central demand of the self‐caring subject. The self‐care paradox, at this level, is not a structural irony. It is a subjective experience of self‐foreclosure.

This dynamic does not press equally on all patients. The achievement subject is one formation among others, and its grip is uneven across clinical populations. It weighs particularly heavily, however, on those whose conditions carry their own moral coding. People managing long‐term addiction, for example, often encounter a recovery discourse in which relapse is read as failure of will, and for whom the decision to contact a clinician mid‐crisis can feel like an admission that one has not been the self‐managing subject recovery requires. Patients with chronic mental illness may internalise parallel expectations of self‐regulation and emotional management, such that seeking acute support registers as a collapse in self‐monitoring rather than a reasonable response to shifting symptoms. Older adults negotiating the threshold of dependence face perhaps the most acute version, caught between a lifetime of independent self‐care and the gradual need to admit limitation; many manage this transition by strenuously performing continued competence, even as difficulty accumulates below the surface. For nursing, these are not peripheral cases. They are the encounters in which the paradox is most likely to remain unspoken, and in which a framework that names the dynamic has the greatest clinical purchase.

For nursing, the implication is uncomfortable. The patient who insists they are managing, who minimises their needs, who apologises for requiring care: this is not a communication failure or a motivational deficit. It is the achievement subject in the clinical encounter. And if nursing theory has no language for this, it cannot respond to it. Worse, it may inadvertently reinforce it, every time self‐care is promoted without attending to the subject it produces.

sec.title: Implications for Nursing Theory and Ethics
Nursing's Complicity and Its Critical Potential
sec.p: Within neoliberal configurations of health, nursing occupies an uncomfortable double position. On one side, nurses are increasingly expected to function as facilitators of self‐care compliance: encouraging responsibility, reinforcing self‐management and supporting patients' alignment with normative expectations of autonomy and risk control. In this role, nursing becomes an instrument through which self‐care discourse is transmitted and enforced at the level of individual encounters (Holmes and Gastaldo 2002).

On the other side, nursing practice occurs precisely at the sites where the limits of self‐care discourse become most visible, where the demand to be self‐sufficient collides with the reality of human vulnerability. That this collision remains largely unarticulated within nursing theory is itself a consequence of the discursive environment this paper has traced (Gastaldo and Holmes 1999). If nursing can name what it witnesses, if it can articulate the self‐care paradox as both a structural and subjective phenomenon, it becomes capable of interrupting the discourse it is otherwise recruited to enforce. The question is whether nursing theory equips nurses to do this, or whether it leaves them complicit by default. The analysis that follows takes the latter possibility seriously, not as a counsel of paralysis, but as the condition under which nursing theory might begin to do better.

sec.title: Reconceptualising the Subjects Nursing Encounters
sec.p: What is required, at the level of nursing theory, is a reconceptualisation of the subjects nursing encounters (Liaschenko and Peter 2004). The patient is not simply a person who needs information about self‐care, nor one who lacks the motivation to manage their health. In many cases, they are a subject who has internalised the demand to be self‐sufficient to the point where acknowledging need feels like failure. The nursing encounter, understood in this light, is not primarily an educational transaction. It is an ethical encounter with a subject whose vulnerability has been turned against them.

This reconceptualisation has practical weight. It asks nurses to attend not only to what patients present (their symptoms, their deficits and their non‐compliance) but to what they withhold: the needs they have learned to regard as inadmissible, the dependence they experience as defeat. The therapeutic significance of being seen as legitimately vulnerable, of having one's care‐seeking affirmed rather than managed, cannot be reduced to technique. It requires a theoretical orientation that takes vulnerability seriously as a condition of human life rather than a problem of self‐management.

This matters because the dominant frameworks through which nurses assess patients are not designed to detect withholding (Levy et al. 2018; Wiseman et al. 2024). Assessment tools, care plans and educational interventions are oriented toward what patients present: their stated needs, their reported symptoms and their expressed willingness to engage. A patient who presents as compliant, self‐managing and reluctant to burden the system will not typically trigger concern. Yet it is precisely this presentation (organised around the imperative to appear sufficient) that the self‐care paradox produces. The subject formed by self‐care discourse does not announce their distress; they perform its absence. Empirical studies of patient non‐disclosure confirm that patients routinely withhold medically relevant information from clinicians, most often out of concern about being judged or appearing difficult (Levy et al. 2019). To respond to this, nursing requires not better assessment instruments but a different theoretical orientation: one that treats the absence of expressed need not as evidence of adequacy, but as a possible sign of its opposite.

This is not a call for nurses to become sceptical of patients' self‐reports or to override their expressed preferences. It is a call for nursing theory to equip nurses with the conceptual resources to recognise a specific pattern: the patient whose self‐sufficiency is performed rather than felt, whose care‐seeking is foreclosed not by circumstance but by the internalised conviction that needing care constitutes a failure of the self. Recognising this pattern does not require a new clinical skill. It requires a theoretical framework that makes the pattern visible, and that is what the present analysis attempts to provide.

To recognise the pattern is a precondition; enacting a response to it is the harder work. Three sets of practices can help to name and interrupt the discourse in the clinical encounter. First, communicative strategies can be reworked so that difficulty is presupposed rather than treated as the exception requiring disclosure. Questions such as ‘How are you managing?’ position the patient as the judge of their own self‐management and invite the affirmative response that the discourse tends to reward. Questions that begin from the assumption that living with this condition is hard, such as ‘What's been getting in the way this week?‘ or ‘What would you find difficult to tell me today?’, redistribute the interpretive burden and make it easier for need to become sayable. Second, relational approaches can explicitly affirm interdependence rather than frame it as deficit. This includes validating the role of family members, neighbours and support networks as part of ongoing care rather than as compensations for self‐care failure, and acknowledging openly within the encounter that reliance on others is ordinary, expected and clinically legitimate. Third, educational and documentation practices can be revised to resist the moral coding that attaches to categories such as ‘non‐adherent’ or ‘non‐compliant’. Where a patient has not followed a treatment plan, nursing notes can describe the circumstances that made the plan difficult to follow, rather than inscribing a characterological judgement that then travels forward through the record and shapes how subsequent clinicians read the patient. None of these practices requires a new clinical skill. They require a theoretical orientation that treats withholding as information about the conditions of the encounter, not as evidence of a well‐managed self.

sec.title: Repositioning Dependence
sec.p: The self‐care paradox invites nursing to return to a question that Orem's framework, for all its limitations, kept open: what does it mean to need care, and what follows from that need? Restoring that ground does not mean returning to Orem uncritically. It means reclaiming the ethical orientation his framework preserved: that dependence is a normal condition of human life, that vulnerability does not disqualify a subject from care and that the need for help is not a moral failure but a relational fact. This orientation has deep roots in feminist care ethics, which has long insisted that dependency and interdependence are not exceptions to the human condition but its constitutive features (Noddings 2013; Sevenhuijsen 2003).

Feminist care ethics offers nursing more than a corrective to neoliberal individualism. It offers a positive account of what care‐oriented practice looks like when dependence is treated as a constitutive feature of human life rather than a failure of self‐management. Within this tradition, the moral significance of care derives not from its capacity to restore individuals to independence, but from its responsiveness to need as such, irrespective of whether that need arises from temporary incapacity or enduring vulnerability (Sevenhuijsen 2003). For nursing, this means that the goal of care is not to produce self‐sufficient subjects who no longer require assistance, but to respond to the full range of human need in ways that affirm rather than penalise dependence.

This orientation finds further support in contemporary accounts of relational autonomy, which understand autonomy not as self‐sufficiency but as a capacity sustained through relationships of care, recognition and support (Mackenzie and Stoljar 2000). Within this framework, the subject is not prior to relationships of care; autonomy itself depends on them. The goal of nursing, understood relationally, is not to produce independent subjects who no longer require care, but to support the conditions under which people can live well with their dependencies: acknowledged, responded to and not experienced as shame. This is a fundamentally different orientation from the one that neoliberal self‐care discourse installs, and recovering it is what the present analysis ultimately asks of nursing theory.

The self‐care paradox, ultimately, names what is lost when this orientation is abandoned. It names the cost (borne invisibly, subjectively, in the interior of subjects who have learned to refuse their own vulnerability) of a discourse that mistakes self‐sufficiency for care. Nursing theory's task is not to resolve this paradox but to refuse to look away from it.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This paper has argued that the problem of self‐care within nursing discourse is not exhausted by its redistribution of responsibility; that much structural critique has already established. The more consequential transformation occurs when self‐care discourse is internalised: when subjects come to govern themselves in its terms, experiencing their own vulnerability as inadequacy and their need for care as personal failure.

The self‐care paradox names what this transformation produces. It is not simply that self‐care has been enlisted in the service of individual responsibilisation; that much has been observed before. What this paper has sought to show is that the paradox operates at a deeper register than structural critique alone can reach. When self‐care discourse is sufficiently internalised, the foreclosure of care‐seeking is no longer produced by institutional barriers or social stigma. It is produced by the subject themselves, by the achievement subject Han describes, who has learned to experience vulnerability as inadequacy and dependence as defeat. The discourse designed to empower individuals to care for themselves becomes, at this level, the mechanism through which they refuse care from others.

This is the self‐care paradox in its fullest form: not an irony of policy, but a condition of subjectivity. And it is this form (interior, self‐imposed and invisible to structural analysis) that nursing theory has not yet adequately confronted.

The analysis developed here has three specific implications for nursing theory. First, it requires that nursing reconceptualise the subjects it encounters: not as patients who need better information or stronger motivation, but as subjects formed by discourse in ways that make their own vulnerability feel inadmissible. Second, it requires that nursing attend to what patients withhold as carefully as to what they present, recognising that the absence of expressed need may signal not adequacy but its suppression. Third, and most fundamentally, it requires that nursing examine its own role in this formation: not as a passive conduit of neoliberal discourse, but as an active participant whose language, assessments and educational interventions contribute to the normative environment in which patients come to understand themselves. Self‐care promotion is not inherently harmful. But self‐care promotion that takes no account of the subject it produces risks reinforcing the very barriers to care it seeks to address.

The implications of this analysis are, in the first instance, theoretical; yet theory is not without consequence for practice. What this paper has argued is that nursing theory must expand its understanding of what it is dealing with when it encounters a patient who cannot seek care, or who will not. The problem may not be informational, motivational or structural. It may be that the patient has been formed, through the accumulated address of self‐care discourse, into a subject for whom needing care feels like a failure of the self. To recognise this is already to change something: it reframes the nursing encounter not as a site of compliance or education, but as an ethical meeting with a subject whose vulnerability has been turned against them. That reframing does not resolve the paradox. But it is the condition under which nursing might begin to respond to it honestly.

To take this seriously is to ask nursing to do something difficult: to remain attentive to the ways its own discourse (including its promotion of self‐care) may participate in producing the very barriers to care it seeks to address. This is not a counsel of paralysis. It is an invitation to a more honest reckoning with what nursing's foundational concepts do when they travel beyond the clinical encounter and into the interior lives of the subjects nursing exists to care for.

sec.title: Use of Artificial Intelligence
sec.p: The authors used Claude (Anthropic) to assist with manuscript editing and structural revision during preparation of this manuscript. All intellectual content, theoretical arguments and final decisions regarding the manuscript remain solely those of the authors.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The authors have nothing to report.

sec.title: Ethics Statement
sec.p: Not applicable. This paper presents a theoretical and philosophical analysis and did not involve human participants, animals or clinical data.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

fn.p: Orem's theory has itself been subject to critique, particularly regarding its individualist assumptions and its grounding in a Western, ability‐centred conception of the self‐caring subject. These critiques are noted here, though a full engagement with them falls outside the scope of the present analysis. The concern of this paper is not to defend Orem's framework as such, but to use it as a conceptual reference point that, despite its limitations, preserves an ethical orientation toward dependence and vulnerability that contemporary self‐care discourse has largely abandoned.

Han's account has been criticised for its tendency toward a unified and ahistorical subject, and for the relative absence of empirical grounding in its claims about contemporary psychological experience. These are legitimate limitations. The present paper does not adopt Han's framework as a sociological description of contemporary subjects, but as a conceptual lens for illuminating a specific dynamic: the way in which internalised demands for performance restructure how individuals relate to their own vulnerability and need. Used in this more limited register, Han's analysis retains considerable explanatory force for the purposes of this argument.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: Data sharing is not applicable to this article as no datasets were generated or analysed during the current study.

sec.p: Data sharing is not applicable to this article as no datasets were generated or analysed during the current study.
